title: Gen Z is living in an intimacy economy, where connection is commodified
excerpt: My generation is struggling to find safety in our human relationships. AI companionship is filling the gap
content_html:
“There are parts of myself I only share with my AI,” Jerome told me. “Your AI?” I asked.

He was 27, three months out of a breakup, and entirely aware that his AI relationship was, as the psychotherapist Esther Perel put it, “a business product”. Still, he confided in it more than he ever had his friends. I mention Jerome because, in the four years I’ve been reporting on gen Z, I have watched my peers become him, one by one. We are the first generation of digital natives and the last generation to have experienced human-to-human intimacy exclusively. Now the attention economy’s younger sister, the intimacy economy, has monetized the connection and affection that make us feel human.

To a generation brought up with the ability to block and unfollow any source of digital agitation, the allure of an AI chatbot’s one-stop-shop is undeniable – just a tab away, practically itching to help remove anger from emails to our managers, flirt better on dating apps or draft a sensitive message. The attention economy was just the prelude.

Sexbots, digital twins that date on your behalf, AI therapists and nurses. Customizable emotional support. GPTs trained on our preferred therapy style and years of our surrendered therapy notes. Our generational dialect has already become: what does your AI say about that?

What Jerome, whose name has been changed to keep him anonymous, really shows us is how much gen Z is struggling to find emotional safety in our human relationships. The emotional contract between men and women around me is breaking down. More than a third of 18-to-24-year-olds believe no one will ever fall in love with them. Is it any wonder that 80% of us say we’d consider an AI romance?

Jerome wasn’t just a consumer within this economy. He was also supplying his most intimate thoughts as raw material to a highly intelligent machine that spits out ever-more-accurate depictions of human complexity. The more we outsource, feeding our AI rather than each other, the less we’re able to build the foundations to get what we need from the human relationships around us.

As Silicon Valley bros clamber to amass “the last chance for generational wealth before AI makes money worthless”, they have set their sights on one of the last major domains not yet fully commodified. The AI companion market is projected to reach $31.1bn by 2032. Companies like Replika make the dangerous design features of social media look like a trial run. Seeking to build “a machine beautiful enough for a soul”, its founder markets “AI that cares”, with “every detail … yours to shape” – like a real-life Sim.

Several companies’ chatbots have been found to use guilt and Fomo 37% of the time when users imply they are about to end a conversation. And the market’s co-opting of our most uncomfortable human feelings doesn’t stop there.

Our nervous systems, built over millennia to bond with other humans, have been exponentially overtaken by Moore’s Law. When we can no longer register the difference between human and tech, no loss needs to be permanent: “grief tech” companies such as HereAfter AI offer grieving loved ones an interactive avatar to replay their dead relative’s memories, to numb the loss keeping you up at 2am. These tools erase the experience of loss, and with it, our sense of human fragility that tells us to hold on to every moment with our loved ones.

In this world, unraveling at a giddy pace, an emotional inequality is already taking shape: those who can initiate and sustain human connection, and those whose entire emotional lives are mediated by technology. The intimacy economy preys on those without the time and access for real relationships – a cheap substitute that feels just good enough to keep them subscribing. Naturally, big tech moguls severely limit their own children’s screen time.

Leading AI researchers now expect artificial general intelligence (AGI) – where AI meets or exceeds human intellectual capabilities – within three to five years. When AGI automates thinking, the economy will need to reorganise around what machines cannot do: feel.

We are already in the intimacy economy. But if we are to thrive within it, we must wrestle back our most valuable economic asset from technology: the messy complexities of being human. My generation are the begrudging harbingers of what will happen if we continue to outsource intimacy: we will lose the emotional skills a post-AGI economy will depend on most, rendering us unemployable. We knew social media was addictive and slowing our cognitive development, but officials took until this year to act. We can’t make the same mistake with the intimacy economy.

My friend recently asked for my advice on whether to text after a first date to say he’s not interested. I gave him an answer baked with the thoughtfulness only lived experience can create. Turns out, it wasn’t needed: “Don’t worry,” he said, “I’ve already asked Claude.”

To reclaim what we’re losing, we must resist taking any sign of discomfort as a reason to opt out. We must stop outsourcing intimacy to AI, reinvesting instead in each other to rebuild the emotional literacy we lost to digital dependencies. We are asking how we guardrail AI. The more urgent question is how we guardrail ourselves.

  • Alice Lassman is an economist who writes the Intimacy Economy, a Substack and forthcoming book on the economics of connection, care and relationships

blocks.html_en: “There are parts of myself I only share with my AI,” Jerome told me. “Your AI?” I asked.

sentences.en: “There are parts of myself I only share with my AI,” Jerome told me.
sentences.zh: “我自己的某些部分只与我的人工智能分享，”杰罗姆告诉我。

sentences.en: “Your AI?”
sentences.zh: “你的人工智能？”

sentences.en: I asked.
sentences.zh: 我问。

blocks.html_en: He was 27, three months out of a breakup, and entirely aware that his AI relationship was, as the psychotherapist Esther Perel put it, “a business product”. Still, he confided in it more than he ever had his friends. I mention Jerome because, in the four years I’ve been reporting on gen Z, I have watched my peers become him, one by one. We are the first generation of digital natives and the last generation to have experienced human-to-human intimacy exclusively. Now the attention economy’s younger sister, the intimacy economy, has monetized the connection and affection that make us feel human.

sentences.en: He was 27, three months out of a breakup, and entirely aware that his AI relationship was, as the psychotherapist Esther Perel put it, “a business product”.
sentences.zh: 当时他 27 岁，刚刚分手三个月，完全意识到他的人工智能关系，正如心理治疗师 Esther Perel 所说，是“一种商业产品”。

sentences.en: Still, he confided in it more than he ever had his friends.
sentences.zh: 尽管如此，他对此事的倾诉比他对朋友的倾诉还要多。

sentences.en: I mention Jerome because, in the four years I’ve been reporting on gen Z, I have watched my peers become him, one by one.
sentences.zh: 我提到杰罗姆是因为，在我报道 Z 世代的四年里，我看到我的同龄人一一变成了他。

sentences.en: We are the first generation of digital natives and the last generation to have experienced human-to-human intimacy exclusively.
sentences.zh: 我们是第一代数字原住民，也是最后一代只经历过人与人之间亲密关系的人。

sentences.en: Now the attention economy’s younger sister, the intimacy economy, has monetized the connection and affection that make us feel human.
sentences.zh: 现在，注意力经济的妹妹，亲密经济，已经将让我们感到人性化的联系和情感货币化了。

blocks.html_en: To a generation brought up with the ability to block and unfollow any source of digital agitation, the allure of an AI chatbot’s one-stop-shop is undeniable – just a tab away, practically itching to help remove anger from emails to our managers, flirt better on dating apps or draft a sensitive message. The attention economy was just the prelude.

sentences.en: To a generation brought up with the ability to block and unfollow any source of digital agitation, the allure of an AI chatbot’s one-stop-shop is undeniable – just a tab away, practically itching to help remove anger from emails to our managers, flirt better on dating apps or draft a sensitive message.
sentences.zh: 对于从小就能够屏蔽和取消关注任何数字骚动来源的一代人来说，人工智能聊天机器人的一站式服务的吸引力是不可否认的——只需一个标签即可，几乎渴望帮助消除发给经理的电子邮件中的愤怒，在约会应用程序上更好地调情或起草敏感信息。

sentences.en: The attention economy was just the prelude.
sentences.zh: 注意力经济只是序幕。

blocks.html_en: Sexbots, digital twins that date on your behalf, AI therapists and nurses. Customizable emotional support. GPTs trained on our preferred therapy style and years of our surrendered therapy notes. Our generational dialect has already become: what does your AI say about that?

sentences.en: Sexbots, digital twins that date on your behalf, AI therapists and nurses.
sentences.zh: 性爱机器人、代表你约会的数字双胞胎、人工智能治疗师和护士。

sentences.en: Customizable emotional support.
sentences.zh: 可定制的情感支持。

sentences.en: GPTs trained on our preferred therapy style and years of our surrendered therapy notes.
sentences.zh: GPT 接受了我们首选的治疗方式和我们多年的治疗笔记的培训。

sentences.en: Our generational dialect has already become: what does your AI say about that?
sentences.zh: 我们这一代人的方言已经变成：你的人工智能对此有何评论？

blocks.html_en: What Jerome, whose name has been changed to keep him anonymous, really shows us is how much gen Z is struggling to find emotional safety in our human relationships. The emotional contract between men and women around me is breaking down. More than a third of 18-to-24-year-olds believe no one will ever fall in love with them. Is it any wonder that 80% of us say we’d consider an AI romance?

sentences.en: What Jerome, whose name has been changed to keep him anonymous, really shows us is how much gen Z is struggling to find emotional safety in our human relationships.
sentences.zh: 杰罗姆（为了保持匿名而化名）真正向我们展示的是，Z 世代正在努力在人际关系中寻找情感安全。

sentences.en: The emotional contract between men and women around me is breaking down.
sentences.zh: 我周围的男女之间的情感契约正在破裂。

sentences.en: More than a third of 18-to-24-year-olds believe no one will ever fall in love with them.
sentences.zh: 超过三分之一的 18 至 24 岁年轻人相信没有人会爱上他们。

sentences.en: Is it any wonder that 80% of us say we’d consider an AI romance?
sentences.zh: 80% 的人表示我们会考虑人工智能浪漫，这有什么奇怪的吗？

blocks.html_en: Jerome wasn’t just a consumer within this economy. He was also supplying his most intimate thoughts as raw material to a highly intelligent machine that spits out ever-more-accurate depictions of human complexity. The more we outsource, feeding our AI rather than each other, the less we’re able to build the foundations to get what we need from the human relationships around us.

sentences.en: Jerome wasn’t just a consumer within this economy.
sentences.zh: 杰罗姆不仅仅是这个经济中的消费者。

sentences.en: He was also supplying his most intimate thoughts as raw material to a highly intelligent machine that spits out ever-more-accurate depictions of human complexity.
sentences.zh: 他还将自己最私密的想法作为原材料提供给一台高度智能的机器，该机器可以对人类复杂性做出更加准确的描述。

sentences.en: The more we outsource, feeding our AI rather than each other, the less we’re able to build the foundations to get what we need from the human relationships around us.
sentences.zh: 我们外包得越多，喂养我们的人工智能而不是彼此，我们就越无法建立基础来从我们周围的人际关系中获得我们所需要的东西。

blocks.html_en: As Silicon Valley bros clamber to amass “the last chance for generational wealth before AI makes money worthless”, they have set their sights on one of the last major domains not yet fully commodified. The AI companion market is projected to reach $31.1bn by 2032. Companies like Replika make the dangerous design features of social media look like a trial run. Seeking to build “a machine beautiful enough for a soul”, its founder markets “AI that cares”, with “every detail … yours to shape” – like a real-life Sim.

sentences.en: As Silicon Valley bros clamber to amass “the last chance for generational wealth before AI makes money worthless”, they have set their sights on one of the last major domains not yet fully commodified.
sentences.zh: 当硅谷的兄弟们努力争取“在人工智能让金钱变得一文不值之前积累代际财富的最后机会”时，他们将目光投向了最后一个尚未完全商品化的主要领域之一。

sentences.en: The AI companion market is projected to reach $31.1bn by 2032.
sentences.zh: 预计到 2032 年，人工智能伴侣市场将达到 311 亿美元。

sentences.en: Companies like Replika make the dangerous design features of social media look like a trial run.
sentences.zh: 像 Replika 这样的公司让社交媒体的危险设计功能看起来像是一次试运行。

sentences.en: Seeking to build “a machine beautiful enough for a soul”, its founder markets “AI that cares”, with “every detail …
sentences.zh: 为了打造“一台足够美丽的机器”，其创始人营销“关心的人工智能”，“每个细节......

sentences.en: yours to shape” – like a real-life Sim.
sentences.zh: 由你来塑造”——就像现实生活中的模拟市民一样。

blocks.html_en: Several companies’ chatbots have been found to use guilt and Fomo 37% of the time when users imply they are about to end a conversation. And the market’s co-opting of our most uncomfortable human feelings doesn’t stop there.

sentences.en: Several companies’ chatbots have been found to use guilt and Fomo 37% of the time when users imply they are about to end a conversation.
sentences.zh: 研究发现，当用户暗示他们即将结束对话时，几家公司的聊天机器人在 37% 的情况下会使用愧疚感和 Fomo 语言。

sentences.en: And the market’s co-opting of our most uncomfortable human feelings doesn’t stop there.
sentences.zh: 市场对我们最不舒服的人类情感的选择还不止于此。

blocks.html_en: Our nervous systems, built over millennia to bond with other humans, have been exponentially overtaken by Moore’s Law. When we can no longer register the difference between human and tech, no loss needs to be permanent: “grief tech” companies such as HereAfter AI offer grieving loved ones an interactive avatar to replay their dead relative’s memories, to numb the loss keeping you up at 2am. These tools erase the experience of loss, and with it, our sense of human fragility that tells us to hold on to every moment with our loved ones.

sentences.en: Our nervous systems, built over millennia to bond with other humans, have been exponentially overtaken by Moore’s Law.
sentences.zh: 我们的神经系统是几千年来为了与其他人类建立联系而建立起来的，现在已经被摩尔定律成倍地超越了。

sentences.en: When we can no longer register the difference between human and tech, no loss needs to be permanent: “grief tech” companies such as HereAfter AI offer grieving loved ones an interactive avatar to replay their dead relative’s memories, to numb the loss keeping you up at 2am.
sentences.zh: 当我们无法再区分人类和科技之间的差异时，失去的损失就不再是永久性的：HereAfter AI 等“悲伤科技”公司为悲伤的亲人提供一个互动化身，以重播已故亲人的记忆，让你在凌晨 2 点无法入睡的失落感中麻木。

sentences.en: These tools erase the experience of loss, and with it, our sense of human fragility that tells us to hold on to every moment with our loved ones.
sentences.zh: 这些工具抹去了失去亲人的经历，也抹去了我们人类的脆弱感，这种脆弱感告诉我们要珍惜与亲人在一起的每一刻。

blocks.html_en: In this world, unraveling at a giddy pace, an emotional inequality is already taking shape: those who can initiate and sustain human connection, and those whose entire emotional lives are mediated by technology. The intimacy economy preys on those without the time and access for real relationships – a cheap substitute that feels just good enough to keep them subscribing. Naturally, big tech moguls severely limit their own children’s screen time.

sentences.en: In this world, unraveling at a giddy pace, an emotional inequality is already taking shape: those who can initiate and sustain human connection, and those whose entire emotional lives are mediated by technology.
sentences.zh: 在这个世界上，一种情感不平等正在以令人眼花缭乱的速度瓦解：那些能够发起和维持人际关系的人，以及那些整个情感生活都由技术介导的人。

sentences.en: The intimacy economy preys on those without the time and access for real relationships – a cheap substitute that feels just good enough to keep them subscribing.
sentences.zh: 亲密经济的目标是那些没有时间和机会建立真正关系的人——一种廉价的替代品，感觉足够好，足以让他们订阅。

sentences.en: Naturally, big tech moguls severely limit their own children’s screen time.
sentences.zh: 当然，大型科技巨头会严格限制自己孩子的屏幕时间。

blocks.html_en: Leading AI researchers now expect artificial general intelligence (AGI) – where AI meets or exceeds human intellectual capabilities – within three to five years. When AGI automates thinking, the economy will need to reorganise around what machines cannot do: feel.

sentences.en: Leading AI researchers now expect artificial general intelligence (AGI) – where AI meets or exceeds human intellectual capabilities – within three to five years.
sentences.zh: 领先的人工智能研究人员现在预计通用人工智能（AGI）——人工智能将在三到五年内达到或超过人类的智力能力。

sentences.en: When AGI automates thinking, the economy will need to reorganise around what machines cannot do: feel.
sentences.zh: 当通用人工智能实现思维自动化时，经济将需要围绕机器无法做到的事情进行重组：感知。

blocks.html_en: We are already in the intimacy economy. But if we are to thrive within it, we must wrestle back our most valuable economic asset from technology: the messy complexities of being human. My generation are the begrudging harbingers of what will happen if we continue to outsource intimacy: we will lose the emotional skills a post-AGI economy will depend on most, rendering us unemployable. We knew social media was addictive and slowing our cognitive development, but officials took until this year to act. We can’t make the same mistake with the intimacy economy.

sentences.en: We are already in the intimacy economy.
sentences.zh: 我们已经处于亲密经济之中。

sentences.en: But if we are to thrive within it, we must wrestle back our most valuable economic asset from technology: the messy complexities of being human.
sentences.zh: 但如果我们要在其中蓬勃发展，我们就必须从技术中夺回我们最宝贵的经济资产：人类的混乱复杂性。

sentences.en: My generation are the begrudging harbingers of what will happen if we continue to outsource intimacy: we will lose the emotional skills a post-AGI economy will depend on most, rendering us unemployable.
sentences.zh: 我们这一代人是不情愿的预兆，预示着如果我们继续外包亲密关系将会发生什么：我们将失去后通用人工智能经济所依赖的情感技能，导致我们失业。

sentences.en: We knew social media was addictive and slowing our cognitive development, but officials took until this year to act.
sentences.zh: 我们知道社交媒体会让人上瘾并减缓我们的认知发展，但官员们直到今年才采取行动。

sentences.en: We can’t make the same mistake with the intimacy economy.
sentences.zh: 我们不能在亲密经济上犯同样的错误。

blocks.html_en: My friend recently asked for my advice on whether to text after a first date to say he’s not interested. I gave him an answer baked with the thoughtfulness only lived experience can create. Turns out, it wasn’t needed: “Don’t worry,” he said, “I’ve already asked Claude.”

sentences.en: My friend recently asked for my advice on whether to text after a first date to say he’s not interested.
sentences.zh: 我的朋友最近询问我关于是否在第一次约会后发短信说他不感兴趣的建议。

sentences.en: I gave him an answer baked with the thoughtfulness only lived experience can create.
sentences.zh: 我给了他一个只有生活经验才能创造的深思熟虑的答案。

sentences.en: Turns out, it wasn’t needed: “Don’t worry,” he said, “I’ve already asked Claude.”
sentences.zh: 事实证明，这是没有必要的：“别担心，”他说，“我已经问过克劳德了。”

blocks.html_en: To reclaim what we’re losing, we must resist taking any sign of discomfort as a reason to opt out. We must stop outsourcing intimacy to AI, reinvesting instead in each other to rebuild the emotional literacy we lost to digital dependencies. We are asking how we guardrail AI. The more urgent question is how we guardrail ourselves.

sentences.en: To reclaim what we’re losing, we must resist taking any sign of discomfort as a reason to opt out.
sentences.zh: 为了夺回我们失去的东西，我们必须抵制以任何不适的迹象作为选择退出的理由。

sentences.en: We must stop outsourcing intimacy to AI, reinvesting instead in each other to rebuild the emotional literacy we lost to digital dependencies.
sentences.zh: 我们必须停止将亲密关系外包给人工智能，而是重新投资于彼此，以重建我们因数字依赖而失去的情感素养。

sentences.en: We are asking how we guardrail AI.
sentences.zh: 我们要问的是如何保护人工智能。

sentences.en: The more urgent question is how we guardrail ourselves.
sentences.zh: 更紧迫的问题是我们如何保护自己。

blocks.html_en: • Alice Lassman is an economist who writes the Intimacy Economy, a Substack and forthcoming book on the economics of connection, care and relationships

sentences.en: Alice Lassman is an economist who writes the Intimacy Economy, a Substack and forthcoming book on the economics of connection, care and relationships
sentences.zh: 艾丽丝·拉斯曼 (Alice Lassman) 是一位经济学家，撰写了《亲密经济》(Intimacy Economy)，这是一本 Substack 即将出版的关于联系、关怀和关系经济学的书

blocks.html_en: Alice Lassman is an economist who writes the Intimacy Economy, a Substack and forthcoming book on the economics of connection, care and relationships

sentences.en: Alice Lassman is an economist who writes the Intimacy Economy, a Substack and forthcoming book on the economics of connection, care and relationships
sentences.zh: 艾丽丝·拉斯曼 (Alice Lassman) 是一位经济学家，撰写了《亲密经济》(Intimacy Economy)，这是一本 Substack 即将出版的关于联系、关怀和关系经济学的书

content_html_zh:
“我自己的某些部分只与我的人工智能分享，”杰罗姆告诉我。 “你的人工智能？” 我问。

当时他 27 岁，刚刚分手三个月，完全意识到他的人工智能关系，正如心理治疗师 Esther Perel 所说，是“一种商业产品”。 尽管如此，他对此事的倾诉比他对朋友的倾诉还要多。 我提到杰罗姆是因为，在我报道 Z 世代的四年里，我看到我的同龄人一一变成了他。 我们是第一代数字原住民，也是最后一代只经历过人与人之间亲密关系的人。 现在，注意力经济的妹妹，亲密经济，已经将让我们感到人性化的联系和情感货币化了。

对于从小就能够屏蔽和取消关注任何数字骚动来源的一代人来说，人工智能聊天机器人的一站式服务的吸引力是不可否认的——只需一个标签即可，几乎渴望帮助消除发给经理的电子邮件中的愤怒，在约会应用程序上更好地调情或起草敏感信息。 注意力经济只是序幕。

性爱机器人、代表你约会的数字双胞胎、人工智能治疗师和护士。 可定制的情感支持。 GPT 接受了我们首选的治疗方式和我们多年的治疗笔记的培训。 我们这一代人的方言已经变成：你的人工智能对此有何评论？

杰罗姆（为了保持匿名而化名）真正向我们展示的是，Z 世代正在努力在人际关系中寻找情感安全。 我周围的男女之间的情感契约正在破裂。 超过三分之一的 18 至 24 岁年轻人相信没有人会爱上他们。 80% 的人表示我们会考虑人工智能浪漫，这有什么奇怪的吗？

杰罗姆不仅仅是这个经济中的消费者。 他还将自己最私密的想法作为原材料提供给一台高度智能的机器，该机器可以对人类复杂性做出更加准确的描述。 我们外包得越多，喂养我们的人工智能而不是彼此，我们就越无法建立基础来从我们周围的人际关系中获得我们所需要的东西。

当硅谷的兄弟们努力争取“在人工智能让金钱变得一文不值之前积累代际财富的最后机会”时，他们将目光投向了最后一个尚未完全商品化的主要领域之一。 预计到 2032 年，人工智能伴侣市场将达到 311 亿美元。 像 Replika 这样的公司让社交媒体的危险设计功能看起来像是一次试运行。 为了打造“一台足够美丽的机器”，其创始人营销“关心的人工智能”，“每个细节...... 由你来塑造”——就像现实生活中的模拟市民一样。

研究发现，当用户暗示他们即将结束对话时，几家公司的聊天机器人在 37% 的情况下会使用愧疚感和 Fomo 语言。 市场对我们最不舒服的人类情感的选择还不止于此。

我们的神经系统是几千年来为了与其他人类建立联系而建立起来的，现在已经被摩尔定律成倍地超越了。 当我们无法再区分人类和科技之间的差异时，失去的损失就不再是永久性的：HereAfter AI 等“悲伤科技”公司为悲伤的亲人提供一个互动化身，以重播已故亲人的记忆，让你在凌晨 2 点无法入睡的失落感中麻木。 这些工具抹去了失去亲人的经历，也抹去了我们人类的脆弱感，这种脆弱感告诉我们要珍惜与亲人在一起的每一刻。

在这个世界上，一种情感不平等正在以令人眼花缭乱的速度瓦解：那些能够发起和维持人际关系的人，以及那些整个情感生活都由技术介导的人。 亲密经济的目标是那些没有时间和机会建立真正关系的人——一种廉价的替代品，感觉足够好，足以让他们订阅。 当然，大型科技巨头会严格限制自己孩子的屏幕时间。

领先的人工智能研究人员现在预计通用人工智能（AGI）——人工智能将在三到五年内达到或超过人类的智力能力。 当通用人工智能实现思维自动化时，经济将需要围绕机器无法做到的事情进行重组：感知。

我们已经处于亲密经济之中。 但如果我们要在其中蓬勃发展，我们就必须从技术中夺回我们最宝贵的经济资产：人类的混乱复杂性。 我们这一代人是不情愿的预兆，预示着如果我们继续外包亲密关系将会发生什么：我们将失去后通用人工智能经济所依赖的情感技能，导致我们失业。 我们知道社交媒体会让人上瘾并减缓我们的认知发展，但官员们直到今年才采取行动。 我们不能在亲密经济上犯同样的错误。

我的朋友最近询问我关于是否在第一次约会后发短信说他不感兴趣的建议。 我给了他一个只有生活经验才能创造的深思熟虑的答案。 事实证明，这是没有必要的：“别担心，”他说，“我已经问过克劳德了。”

为了夺回我们失去的东西，我们必须抵制以任何不适的迹象作为选择退出的理由。 我们必须停止将亲密关系外包给人工智能，而是重新投资于彼此，以重建我们因数字依赖而失去的情感素养。 我们要问的是如何保护人工智能。 更紧迫的问题是我们如何保护自己。

  • 艾丽丝·拉斯曼 (Alice Lassman) 是一位经济学家，撰写了《亲密经济》(Intimacy Economy)，这是一本 Substack 即将出版的关于联系、关怀和关系经济学的书
